nodes.label: Contemporary Seasonal RF
nodes.description: Average rainfall for the dry season (April to October) and wet season (November to May), based on the 30-year contemporary climatological period from 1991 to 2020.

nodes.label: Seasonal Tmax
nodes.description: Average maximum temperature for the dry season (April to October) and wet season (November to May), based on the 30-year contemporary climatological period from 1991 to 2020.

nodes.label: Seasonal Tmin
nodes.description: Average minimum temperature for the dry season (April to October) and wet season (November to May), based on the 30-year contemporary climatological period from 1991 to 2020.

nodes.label: Seasonal Tmean
nodes.description: Average mean temperature for the dry season (April to October) and wet season (November to May), based on the 30-year contemporary climatological period from 1991 to 2020.

nodes.label: Monthly
nodes.description: Monthly rainfall from January 1920 to December 2012. Station rainfall data were interpolated using ordinary kriging.

nodes.label: Annual (Calendar Year)
nodes.description: Total rainfall accumulated over a calendar year (January to December), from 1990 to last (or previous?) year.

nodes.label: Annual (Water Year)
nodes.description: Total rainfall accumulated over a water year (October to September), from 1991 to last (or previous?) year.

nodes.label: Monthly Value
nodes.description: Monthly rainfall from January 1990 to Previous month. These maps represent the amount of rainfall from midnight on the first of the target month to midnight on the last day of the month. Station rainfall data are interpolated using an automated optimized climate aided ordinary kriging methodology see Lucas etal 2022.

nodes.label: Monthly Anom
nodes.description: Monthly rainfall anomaly from January 1990 to previous month. These maps represent the amount or percent difference of the station rainfall data interpolated using an automated optimized climate aided ordinary kriging from the expected mean monthly rainfall map as defined by Giambelluca etal XX.

nodes.label: Daily
nodes.description: Dailyrainfall from January 1 1990 to Previous day. These maps represent the amount of rainfall from midnight on the target day to midnight at the end of the day. Station rainfall data are interpolated using an automated optimized climate aided ordinary kriging methodology

nodes.label: Annual
nodes.description: Year-averaged minimum daily temperature from 1990 to the previous year. These maps represent the average minimum temperature in a day during each year in the record. The monthly minimum temperature maps are averaged over each year to produce the Annual Minimum Temperature.

nodes.label: Monthly
nodes.description: Monthly-averaged minimum daily temperature from January 1990 to the previous month. These maps represent the average minimum temperature in a day during each month in the record. The daily minimum temperature maps are averaged over each month to produce the Monthly Minimum Temperature.

nodes.label: Daily
nodes.description: Daily minimum temperature from January 1, 1990 to the previous day. These maps represent the minimum temperature over the 24-hour period starting from midnight (00:00) on the target day to midnight at the end of day. The minimum temperature observations from all available weather stations on the target day are regressed against elevation to produce a linear model, which is used to estimate the minimum temperature at each location.

nodes.label: Annual
nodes.description: Year-averaged maximum daily temperature from 1990 to the previous year. These maps represent the average maximum temperature in a day during each year in the record. The monthly maximum temperature maps are averaged over each year to produce the Annual Maximum Temperature.

nodes.label: Monthly
nodes.description: Monthly-averaged maximum daily temperature from January 1990 to the previous month. These maps represent the average maximum temperature in a day during each month in the record. The daily maximum temperature maps are averaged over each month to produce the Monthly Maximum Temperature.

nodes.label: Daily
nodes.description: Daily maximum temperature from January 1, 1990 to the previous day. These maps represent the maximum temperature over the 24-hour period starting from midnight (00:00) on the target day to midnight at the end of day. The maximum temperature observations from all available weather stations on the target day are regressed against elevation to produce a linear model, which is used to estimate the maximum temperature at each location.

nodes.label: Annual
nodes.description: Year-averaged daily temperature from 1990 to the previous year. These maps represent the average temperature in a day during each year in the record. The monthly average temperature maps are averaged over each year to produce the Annual Average Temperature.

nodes.label: Monthly
nodes.description: Monthly-averaged temperature from January 1990 to the previous month. These maps represent the average temperature in a day during each month in the record. The daily average temperature maps are averaged over each month to produce the Monthly Average Temperature.

nodes.label: Daily
nodes.description: Daily average temperature from January 1, 1990 to the previous day. These maps represent the average temperature over the 24-hour period starting from midnight (00:00) on the target day to midnight at the end of day. The maximum and minimum temperature maps for the target day are averaged to produce the Daily Average Temperature.

nodes.label: 2:00 PM Avg.
nodes.description: These maps represent the relative humidity averaged from 1:00PM to 1:59PM each day from January 2002 to the previous day. Relative humidity is estimated using a regression spline model of station observations of dew point temperature predicted by elevation and gridded maximum daily temperature.

nodes.label: 1 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-1 represents a 1-month timescale and is useful for assessing short-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 3 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-3 represents a 3-month timescale and is useful for assessing short to medium-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 6 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-6 represents a 6-month timescale and is useful for assessing medium-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 9 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-9 represents a 9-month timescale and is useful for assessing medium to long-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 12 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-12 represents a 12-month timescale and is useful for assessing long-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 24 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-24 represents a 24-month timescale and is useful for assessing long-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 36 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-36 represents a 36-month timescale and is useful for assessing long-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.

nodes.label: 64 Month
nodes.description: Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) is a precipitation-based drought product. SPI-64 represents a 64-month timescale and is useful for assessing long-term conditions. Negative SPI values indicate drier-than-normal conditions, with values below -1.0 suggesting moderate to severe drought. Positive values reflect wetter-than-normal conditions.
